agent1_snippets.title: Selenium Deficiency -- Continuing Education Activity
agent1_snippets.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors associated with selenium deficiency in patients, including dietary habits, geographical location, and underlying medical conditions. Screen individuals at risk for selenium deficiency by conducting thorough medical history assessments and ordering appropriate laboratory tests to measure selenium levels promptly. Select appropriate selenium supplementation options, such as selenomethionine or selenium-enriched foods, tailored to each patient's specific needs and preferences. Communicate effectively with patients about the diagnosis, treatment, and management of selenium deficiency, providing education on dietary sources and supplementation options. agent1_snippets.title: Cystinuria -- Pearls and Other Issues -- Optional Therapies
agent1_snippets.content: Alpha-lipoic acid is readily available, well-tolerated, safe, and might be of real value based on very good results in limited studies. Selenium is cheap and easy to administer, but its role is unproven and untested except for a single study showing a benefit. Bucillamine might be a better, more effective, and less toxic thiol drug for cystinuria, but it remains mostly untested for this indication. Availability is also an issue.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Besides 20 amino acids that participate in protein synthesis, recently, 2 more new amino acids have been described—selenocysteine and pyrrolysine. Selenocysteine occurs at the active site of several enzymes, including thioredoxin reductase and glutathione peroxidase. Pyrrolysine is not present in humans but is used in the biosynthesis of proteins in some methanogenic species, such as archaea and bacteria. [1] [2] [3]

agent1_snippets.title: Selenium -- Administration
agent1_snippets.content: The most efficient way to obtain selenium systemically is through a well-balanced diet. Food items rich in selenium include Brazil nuts, halibut, sardines, yellowfin tuna, and shrimp, as well as turkey, chicken, and beef. If additional supplementation is needed, selenium administration can be through both oral and parenteral routes, and the preferred route depends on the indication for use. Most cases of selenium deficiency will reverse with oral supplements or selenium-containing foods. Intravenous infusions of selenium are available; however, they must be diluted before injection to prevent tissue irritation. [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Essential Amino Acids -- Fundamentals
agent1_snippets.content: Approximately 20,000 unique protein-encoding genes are responsible for more than 100,000 unique proteins in the human body. Although hundreds of amino acids are found in nature, only about 20 are necessary to synthesize all the proteins in the human body and most other life forms. These 20 amino acids are all L-isomer, α-amino acids. All amino acids, except for glycine, contain a chiral αcarbon. All these amino acids are L-isomers with an R-absolute configuration except for glycine and cysteine. Glycine lacks a chiral center, whereas cysteine has an S-absolute configuration due to its sulfur-containing R-group. Selenocysteine and pyrrolysine are considered the 21st and 22nd amino acids, respectively. More recently discovered amino acids may be incorporated into protein chains during ribosomal protein synthesis. Although pyrrolysine has biological functionality, humans do not incorporate it into protein synthesis. When translated, these 22 amino acids may also be modified through a post-translational modification to add further diversity in generating proteins. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Selenium -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: In excessive doses, selenium is toxic, but it is an essential micronutrient in humans. Selenium supplementation is indicated to replete stores in cases of selenium deficiency. However, this is usually secondary to the localized diet versus an individual problem with the patient. Therefore, a common way to reduce selenium deficiency is via biofortification at the agricultural level. [1] A therapeutic option is to add selenium-enriched foods into the diet on an individualized level. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Selenium -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Managing selenium deficiency can be difficult, considering this phenomenon typically occurs in underdeveloped and developing regions of the world where healthcare and nutrition are limited. Selenium levels are rarely tested, and clinicians and other healthcare providers are often unaware of deficiencies and treatment options. Therefore, the entire interprofessional healthcare team needs to work together, starting with gathering a good history from the patient. Along with physicians, mid-level practitioners (PAS and NPS), nurses, medical assistants, pharmacists, nutritionists/dieticians, and technicians can ask questions and collect data from the patient. Specifically, it is essential to ask about diet, as many selenium-deficient patients lack selenium in the food they consume. Pharmacists must stay up to date with the most recent guidelines regarding selenium supplementation dosing so that pharmacists and clinical staff can communicate to ensure the most beneficial course of action for patients.

agent1_snippets.content: Parenteral therapy is also preferred in patients who are noncompliant with oral therapy or those with altered gastrointestinal anatomy. Patients with an irreversible cause of cobalamin deficiency, such as pernicious anemia and bariatric surgery, require supplementation for life. On the other hand, patients with reversible causes of deficiency (drug-induced, dietary deficiency) are treated until the deficiency is corrected.

agent1_snippets.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: The treatment for SCD of the spinal cord is the supplementation of vitamin B12 either in oral or parenteral form. The underlying cause of vitamin B12 deficiency determines the specific route and duration of therapy. Patients with Crohn disease and celiac disease should be monitored for signs and symptoms, and declining levels of vitamin B12 levels. In patients with chronic conditions that can deplete vitamin B12 levels, such as celiac disease, pernicious anemia, and Crohn disease post-resection, indefinite supplementation of vitamin B12 is warranted. [25] The dose of vitamin B12, route of administration, and duration of treatment depend on the presenting symptoms, the urgency of treatment, the underlying etiology, and the patient's preference. [15]

agent1_snippets.title: Hyperhomocysteinemia -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Homocysteine is an amino acid not supplied by the diet that can be converted into cysteine or recycled into methionine, an essential amino acid, with the aid of specific B vitamins. Homocysteine levels vary between men and women, with a normal range typically between 5 to 15 micromol/L. Hyperhomocysteinemia is when levels exceed 15 micromol/L. [1]

agent1_snippets.title: Spinal Cord Subacute Combined Degeneration -- Deterrence and Patient Education
agent1_snippets.content: SCD of the spinal cord is a preventable and treatable disease. Prophylactic vitamin B12 administration is not routinely recommended and is only indicated in specific instances. Individuals who adhere to a strict vegan diet may deplete their vitamin B12 stores in 2 to 3 years due to inadequate dietary intake and hence require routine oral supplementation. This is especially important during preconceptional counseling of women who are strictly vegan, as the developing fetus also needs vitamin B12.

By encompassing methyltestosteron, the terminology acknowledges anabolic‑androgenic agents used in hormone therapy; through sacubitril / valsartan, it reflects modern pharmacologic strategies that target both neprilysin inhibition and angiotensin‑II receptor blockade for cardiovascular disease; the inclusion of seleničitan sodný highlights trace‑element compounds that may be used as supplements or in specific therapeutic contexts; and the entry for septicemia (except in labor) delineates systemic bacterial infection while explicitly excluding obstetric‑related cases. Multivitamin‑plus‑mineral preparations integrate these vitamins with essential minerals (calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide, copper oxide, magnesium‑L‑threonate, phosphorus, copper, zinc, calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate) and bioactive compounds (essential fatty acids, probiotics, NADH, flavin adenine dinucleotide, phosphatidylserine, betaine, succinic acid). Folic acid (kyselina listová) is a central component of virtually all IRON/MULTIVATIMENS products and is frequently combined with iron, vitamin B complexes, vitamin C, vitamin A, vitamin D, calcium, magnesium, zinc, essential fatty acids, probiotics, docusate and other trace elements. Specific examples that list folic acid include the aforementioned bariatric multivitamins, the complex “BETA CAROTENE 2850 [iU]/ASCORBIC ACID 120 mg … IRON SUCROSE 1.35 mg / IODINE 0.223 mg” (Bal‑Care DHA Essential and Bal‑Care DHA), FA 1 mg/IRON (FEFM‑FEPOL) 106 mg/multivitamins CAP, the delayed‑release capsule “LEUCOVORIN 2.5 mg / FOLIC ACID 1 mg / … MAGNESIUM ASCORBATE 24 mg / ZINC ASCORBATE 1 mg …” (EnLyte), and the “FOLIC ACID/IRON/MULTIVITAMINS” blend. agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2096
agent2_snippets.content: The concept “Measurement of substance” is linked to the specific test “السيلنيوم (++Se)” through an inverse‑is‑a relationship, indicating that the selenium measurement is a particular type of substance measurement. The selenium measurement itself is further classified by another inverse‑is‑a relationship as belonging to the broader, though undefined, category “كيمياء غير محدد” (unspecified chemistry). This unspecified chemistry category is connected by a has‑parent relationship to the more detailed assay “叶酸+氰钴胺素:印象:时间点:血清/全血:名义型,” which represents a folic acid and cyanocobalamin test evaluated at a specific time point in serum or whole‑blood specimens. Together, these relationships place the selenium measurement within a hierarchical framework of chemical laboratory testing, linking it from a general measurement of substances to an undefined chemistry group and ultimately to a specific folate‑cobalamin assay context.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 4 -- Community 2867
agent2_snippets.content: Acyclovir, benztropine, biotin, demethylchlortetracycline, diethylstilbestrol, paclitaxel, hexachlorofen, and sodium selenate are all identified as drugs that are contraindicated in the presence of drug hypersensitivity (فرط الحساسية الدوائية). This indicates that each of these agents should be avoided or used with extreme caution in patients who have a known allergic reaction to medications, as they carry a risk of provoking or worsening hypersensitivity responses. Recognizing this common contraindication across a diverse group of agents—ranging from antiviral and anticholinergic medications to vitamins, antibiotics, hormonal agents, chemotherapeutic drugs, chlorinated pesticides, and selenium compounds—helps clinicians prevent adverse drug reactions and ensures safer prescribing practices for individuals with a history of drug allergy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 2 -- Community 334
agent2_snippets.content: Selenium is classified as a mineral element and is considered to belong to the broader group of heavy metals. As a mineral, selenium is the specific substance referenced by the term “minerály” as its parent category. Selenium supplements contain selenium, providing this trace element in a supplemental form for nutritional or therapeutic use. Understanding selenium’s classification as a mineral related to heavy metals and its availability through supplements is important for assessing its role in health and for guiding appropriate patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 104
agent2_snippets.content: Selen is classified as a mineral and is placed within the broader category of heavy metals (těžké kovy). Selenium supplements are directly linked to selen and are employed in selenium supplement therapy, which is described as a therapeutic approach intended, among other uses, to support calcium supplements (مكملات الكالسيوم). The administration of prophylactic beta‑blockers and beta‑adrenergic receptor blocking agent therapy are identified as specific methods of drug delivery, illustrating how particular therapeutic actions are organized. Calcium supplements themselves are also noted as a therapeutic intent, underscoring their role in patient care. Together, these relationships demonstrate how selen, as a mineral and heavy metal, is utilized in supplement form for therapeutic interventions, while beta‑blocker administration and calcium supplementation represent additional, method‑based therapeutic strategies.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1490
agent2_snippets.content: Methylprednisolone, a corticosteroid medication, may be used to treat multiple sclerosis, offering anti‑inflammatory and immunosuppressive effects that can help reduce disease activity. In addition, vitamin B12 is also considered a potential therapeutic agent for multiple sclerosis, as supplementation may support neurological function and address deficiencies that can exacerbate symptoms. Both methylprednisolone and vitamin B12 represent possible treatment options in the management of multiple sclerosis, highlighting the importance of a multifaceted therapeutic approach.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 597
agent2_snippets.content: Iron‑containing multivitamins are designed to supply a broad spectrum of essential nutrients in a single dosage form, often for patients with increased micronutrient needs such as those who have undergone bariatric or malabsorptive surgery. The iron component is provided as polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, iron sucrose, or ferric pyrophosphate, frequently combined with a full vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin B12, and folic acid). Vitamin C is added to enhance iron absorption, while vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), beta‑carotene and DHA support visual, skeletal and neurological health. Mineral supplementation includes calcium (as calcium carbonate, calcium ascorbate or calcium threonate), magnesium, zinc, copper and iodine to counteract postoperative deficiencies. Additional excipients such as succinic acid, docusate, essential fatty acids and probiotics are incorporated to improve
